n0701335: tn Heb “so we can prepare before you a young goat of the goats.”
n0701337: tn The words “he said this” are supplied in the translation for clarification. Manoah should have known from these words that the angel represented the Lord. In the preceding narrative the narrator has informed the reader that the visitor is the angel of the Lord, but Manoah and his wife did not perceive this. In vv. 5 and 7 the angel refers to “God” (‏אֱלֹהִים‎, ʾelohim), not the Lord (‏יְהוַה‎, yehvah). Manoah’s wife calls the visitor “a man sent from God” and “God’s angel” (v. 6), while Manoah prays to the “Lord” (‏אֲדוֹנָי‎, ʾadonay) and calls the visitor “a man sent from God” (v. 8).
n0701338: tn Heb “Who your name? For [when] your word comes [to pass], we will honor you.” Manoah apparently gets tongue-tied and uses the wrong pronoun (“who” instead of “what”). He starts to say, “Who are you?” But then he switches to “your name” as if he began the sentence with “what.” See R. G. Boling, Judges (AB), 222.
n0701339: tn Heb “Why do you ask for my name, for it is incomprehensible?” The Hebrew adjective e ‏פִּלְאִי‎ (pilʾi, “wonderful, incomprehensible”) refers to what is in a category of its own and is beyond full human understanding. Note the use of this word in Ps 139:6, where God’s knowledge is described as incomprehensible and unattainable.
n0701340: tc Heb “Doing an extraordinary deed while Manoah and his wife were watching.” The subject of the participle is missing. The translation assumes that the phrase “the Lord’s messenger” was lost by homoioteleuton. If the text originally read ‏לַיהוָה מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה‎ (layhvah malʾakh yehvah), the scribe’s eye could have jumped from the first ‏יְהוָה‎ to the second, accidentally omitting two of the three words. Later the conjunction ‏וּ‎ (shureq) would have been added to the following ‏מַפְלִא‎ (mafliʾ) for syntactical reasons. Another possibility is that a pronominal subject (‏הוּא‎, huʾ) has been lost in the MT due to haplography.
n0701343: tn Or “seen God.” Some take the Hebrew term ‏אֱלֹהִים‎ (ʾelohim) as the divine name (“God”) here, but this seems unlikely since v. 21 informs us that Manoah realized this was the Lord’s messenger, not God himself. Of course, he may be exaggerating for the sake of emphasis. Another option, the one followed in the translation, understands Manoah to be referring to a lesser deity. The term ‏אֱלֹהִים‎ (ʾelohim) is sometimes used of an individual deity other than the Lord (see BDB 43 s.v. 2.a). One cannot assume that Manoah was a theologically sophisticated monotheist.
n0701345: tn Heb “the woman.” For clarity this has been specified in the translation as “Manoah’s wife.”
n0701346: tn The name appears to mean “sun-like” or “solar.”
n0701348: tn Or “move him to action”; or “stir him.”
n070141: tn Heb “and he saw a woman in Timnah, one of the daughters of the Philistines.”
n070143: tn Heb “I have seen a woman in Timnah, one of the daughters of the Philistines.”
n070144: tn Heb “my.” The singular may seem strange, since the introduction to the quotation attributes the words to his father and mother. But Samson’s father apparently speaks for both himself and his wife. However, the Lucianic recension of the LXX and the Syriac Peshitta have a second person pronoun here (“you”), and this may represent the original reading.
n070145: tn Heb “Is there not among the daughters of your brothers or among all my people a woman that you have to go to get a wife among the uncircumcised Philistines?”
n070146: tn “Her” is first in the Hebrew word order for emphasis. Samson wanted this Philistine girl, no one else. See C. F. Burney, Judges, 357.
n070147: tn Heb “because she is right in my eyes.”
n070149: tn Heb “for an opportunity he was seeking from the Philistines.”
n0701410: tc The Hebrew includes “and his father and his mother.” See the next note.
n0701411: tc The MT reads “they approached,” while the LXX reads “he approached.” The previous sentence suggests that his parents were there, reading literally, “he went down, Samson and his father and his mother, to Timnah.” But the story line suggests that his parents were not there, as v. 6b reports that Samson did not tell them about the incident. The following sentence begins with ‏וְהִנֵּה‎ (vehinneh, “and behold”). This particle is used to focus or shift attention, typically pointing something out or introducing it into the scene (here the lion). But the scene that ‏וְהִנֵּה‎ comments on is set by the previous verb. If the verb “approached” were plural, then Samson’s parents should be with him when the lion attacks, something that contradicts the story as a whole. This indicates the verb should be singular. Since the previous verb, “went down,” is also singular (so also v. 7a), the phrase “and his father and his mother” may have been accidentally copied into the text under the influence of v. 4a. Later the verb was changed to “they approached” to account for the addition, but not until after the LXX was translated. Or one might suppose that his parents had gone on this trip down to Timnah (retaining “and his father and his mother”), but he had separated from them before approaching to the vineyards.
n0701412: tn Heb “and look, a young lion of the lions was roaring to meet him.”
n0701414: tn Heb “him” or “it”; the referent (the lion) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n0701415: tn Heb “and there was nothing in his hand.”
n0701417: tn Heb “She was the right one in the eyes of Samson.”
n0701420: tn Heb “went.” Samson apparently went home to his parents before going to Timnah for the marriage. Seeing and tasting the honey appears to encourage Manoah to go with his son to Timnah. Perhaps both Samson and his father viewed the honey as a good omen of future blessing. Possibly Samson considered it a symbol of sexual pleasure or an aphrodisiac. Note the use of honey imagery in Song 4:11 and 5:1.
n0701421: sn Touching the carcass of a dead animal undoubtedly violated Samson’s Nazirite status. See Num 6:6.
n0701422: tn Heb “And his father went down to the woman.”
n0701425: tn Heb “When they saw him, they gave him thirty companions and they were with him.” Instead of ‏כִּרְאוֹתָם‎ (kirʾotam, “when they saw”) some ancient witnesses (e.g., some mss of the LXX) assume the reading ‏בְּיִרְאָתָם‎ (beyirʾatam, “because they feared”).
n0701426: tn Heb “If you really can tell it to me [during] the seven days of the feast and you find [its answer].”
n0701429: tn Heb “Give your riddle so we can hear it.”
n0701430: tc The MT reads “seventh.” In Hebrew there is a difference of only one letter between the words ‏רְבִיעִי‎ (reviʿi, “fourth”) and ‏שְׁבִיעִי‎ (sheviʿi, “seventh”). Some ancient textual witnesses (e.g., LXX and the Syriac Peshitta) read “fourth,” here, which certainly harmonizes better with the preceding verse (cf. “for three days”) and with v. 17. Another option is to change ‏שְׁלֹשֶׁת‎ (sheloshet, “three”) at the end of v. 14 to ‏שֵׁשֶׁת‎ (sheshet, “six”), but the resulting scenario does not account as well for v. 17, which implies the bride had been hounding Samson for more than one day.
n0701431: tn Heb “Entice your husband so that he might tell us the riddle.”
n0701433: tn The Hebrew text expands the statement: “burn up with fire.” The words “with fire” are redundant in English and have been omitted from the translation for stylistic reasons.
n0701435: tc The translation assumes the Hebrew form ‏הֲלֹם‎ (halom, “here,” attested in five Hebrew mss and supported by the Targum), instead of the inexplicable ‏הֲלֹא‎ (haloʾ), a negative particle with interrogative particle prefixed to it.
n0701436: tn For discussion of this difficult form, see C. F. Burney, Judges, 364.
n0701442: tn Heb “the seven days [during] which they held the party.” This does not mean she cried for the entire seven days; v. 15 indicates otherwise. She cried for the remainder of the seven day period, beginning on the fourth day.
n0701444: tn Heb “she told the riddle to the sons of her people.”
n0701445: sn Plowed with my heifer. This statement emphasizes that the Philistines had utilized a source of information which should have been off-limits to them. Heifers were used in plowing (Hos 10:11), but one typically used one’s own farm animals, not another man’s.
n0701448: tn Heb “he went up to his father’s house.”
n0701449: tn Heb “to his companion who had been his attendant.”
n070151: sn The wheat harvest took place during the month of May. See O. Borowski, Agriculture in Iron Age Israel, 37, 88.
n070152: tn Heb “Samson visited his wife with a young goat.”
n070153: tn The words “to her father” are supplied in the translation (see the end of the verse).
n070154: tn Heb “I want to approach.” The verb ‏בּוֹא‎ (boʾ) with the preposition ‏אֶל‎ (ʾel) means “come to” or “approach,” but is also used as a euphemism for sexual relations.
n070155: tn Heb “I will go to my wife in the bedroom.” The Hebrew idiom ‏בּוֹא אֶל‎ (boʾ ʾel, “to go to”) often has sexual connotations. The cohortative form used by Samson can be translated as indicating resolve (“I want to go”) or request (“let me go”).
n070156: tn Heb “saying, I said.” The first person form of ‏אָמַר‎ (ʾamar, “to say”) sometimes indicates self-reflection. The girl’s father uses the infinitive absolute for emphasis.
n070157: tn Heb “hating, you hated.” Once again the girl’s father uses the infinitive absolute for emphasis.
n070158: tn Heb “Is her younger sister not better than her? Let her [i.e., the younger sister] be yours instead of her [i.e., Samson’s ‘bride’]).”
n070159: tc Codex Alexandrinus (A) of the (original) LXX has the singular “to him.”
n0701510: tn Heb “I am innocent this time from the Philistines when I do with them harm.”
n0701512: tn Heb “He turned tail to tail and placed one torch between the two tails in the middle.”
n0701515: tn Heb “and they said.” The subject of the plural verb is indefinite.
n0701516: tn Heb “he”; the referent (the Timnite) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n0701517: tn Heb “his”; the referent (Samson) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n0701518: tn The Hebrew text expands the statement with the additional phrase “burned with fire.” The words “with fire” are redundant in English and have been omitted from the translation for stylistic reasons. Some textual witnesses read “burned…her father’s house,” perhaps under the influence of 14:15. On the other hand, the shorter text may have lost this phrase due to haplography.
n0701519: tn The Niphal of ‏נָקָם‎ (naqam, “to avenge, to take vengeance”) followed by the preposition ‏ב‎ (bet) has the force “to get revenge against.” See 1 Sam 18:25; Jer 50:15; Ezek 25:12.
n0701521: tn Heb “He struck them, calf on thigh, [with] a great slaughter.” The precise meaning of the phrase “calf on thigh” is uncertain.
n0701523: tn Or “spread out.” The Niphal of ‏נָטָשׁ‎ (natash) has this same sense in 2 Sam 5:18, 22.
n0701525: tn Heb “they”; the referent (the Philistines) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n0701527: tn Heb “meet [with hostility]”; “harm.” In light of v. 13, “kill” is an appropriate translation.
n0701528: tn Heb “No,” meaning that they will not harm him.
n0701534: tn Heb “he reached out his hand and took it.”
n0701535: tn The Hebrew text adds “with it.” This has not been included in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n0701536: tn The precise meaning of the second half of the line (‏חֲמוֹר חֲמֹרָתָיִם‎, khamor khamoratayim) is uncertain. The present translation assumes that the phrase means, “a heap, two heaps” and refers to the heaps of corpses littering the battlefield. Other options include: (a) “I have made donkeys of them” (cf. NIV; see C. F. Burney, Judges, 373, for a discussion of this view, which understands a denominative verb from the noun “donkey”); (b) “I have thoroughly skinned them” (see HALOT 330 s.v. IV cj. ‏חמר‎, which appeals to an Arabic cognate for support); (c) “I have stormed mightily against them,” which assumes the verb ‏חָמַר‎ (khamar, “to ferment; to foam; to boil up”).
n0701538: sn The name Ramath Lehi means “Height of the Jawbone.”
n0701539: tn Heb “you have placed into the hand of your servant.”
n0701540: tn Heb “the hand of uncircumcised.” “Hand” often represents power or control. “The uncircumcised [ones]” is used as a pejorative and in the context refers to the Philistines.
n0701541: tn The word translated “basin” refers to a circular-shaped depression in the land’s surface.
n0701543: tn Heb “named it”; the referent (the spring) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n0701544: sn The name En Hakkore means “Spring of the one who cries out.”
n070161: tn Heb “approached her.” The idiom ‏בּוֹא אֶל‎ (boʾ ʾel, “to go to”) is a euphemism for sexual relations.
n070162: tc Heb “To the Gazites, saying.” A verb is missing from the MT; some ancient Greek witnesses add “it was reported.”
n070163: tn Heb “And they surrounded.” The rest of the verse suggests that “the town” is the object, not “the house.” Though the Gazites knew Samson was in the town, apparently they did not know exactly where he had gone. Otherwise, they could have just gone into or surrounded the house and would not have needed to post guards at the city gate.
n070164: tn Heb “and they lay in wait for him all night in the city gate.”
n070167: tn The words “He will not leave” are supplied in the translation for clarification.
n070169: tn Heb “And Samson lay until the middle of the night and arose in the middle of the night.”
n0701611: tn Heb “which is upon the face of Hebron.”
n0701612: tn Heb “subdue him in order to humiliate him.”
n0701613: tn Heb “how you can be subdued in order to be humiliated.”
n0701615: tn The word refers to a bowstring, probably made from animal tendons. See Ps 11:2; Job 30:11.
n0701616: tn Heb “And the ones lying in wait were sitting for her.” The grammatically singular form ‏וְהָאֹרֵב‎ (vehaʾorev) is collective here, referring to the rulers as a group (so also in v. 16).
n0701620: tn See Gen 31:7; Exod 8:29 [8:25 HT]; Job 13:9; Isa 44:20; Jer 9:4 for other uses of this Hebrew word (II ‏תָּלַל‎, talal), which also occurs in v. 13.
n0701621: tn Heb “with which no work has been done.”
n0701623: tn Heb “And the ones lying in wait were sitting in the bedroom.”
n0701624: tn Heb “them”; the referent (the ropes) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n0701625: tn Heb “head” (also in the following verse). By metonymy the head is mentioned in the Hebrew text in place of the hair on it.
n0701626: tn Heb “with the web.” For a discussion of how Delilah did this, see C. F. Burney, Judges, 381, and G. F. Moore, Judges (ICC), 353-54.
n0701628: tc The MT of vv. 13b-14a reads simply, “He said to her, ‘If you weave the seven braids of my head with the web.’ And she fastened with the pin and said to him.” The additional words in the translation, “and secure it with the pin, I will become weak and be like any other man.’ 16:14 So she made him go to sleep, wove the seven braids of his hair into the fabric on the loom,” which without doubt represent the original text, are supplied from the ancient Greek version. (In both vv. 13b and 14a the Greek version has “to the wall” after “with the pin,” but this is an interpretive addition that reflects a misunderstanding of ancient weaving equipment. See G. F. Moore, Judges [ICC], 353-54.) The Hebrew textual tradition was accidentally shortened during the copying process. A scribe’s eye jumped from the first instance of “with the web” to the second, causing him to leave out inadvertently the intervening words.
n0701629: tn The Hebrew adds, “from his sleep.” This has not been included in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n0701630: tn Heb “when your heart is not with me.”
n0701632: tn Heb “and his spirit was short [i.e., impatient] to the point of death.”
n0701634: tn Heb “a razor has not come upon my head.”
n0701635: tn Or “set apart to God.” Traditionally the Hebrew term ‏נָזִיר‎ (nazir) has been translated “Nazirite.” The word is derived from the verb ‏נָזַר‎ (nazar, “to dedicate; to consecrate; to set apart”).
n0701637: tn Heb “I.” The referent has been made more specific in the translation (“my head”).
n0701640: tc The translation follows the Qere, ‏לִי‎ (li, “to me”) rather than the Kethib, ‏לָהּ‎ (lah, “to her”).
n0701642: tn Heb “on her knees.” The expression is probably euphemistic for sexual intercourse. See HALOT 160-61 s.v. ‏בֶּרֶךְ‎.
n0701643: tn Heb “she called for a man and she shaved off.” The point seems to be that Delilah acted through the instrumentality of the man. See J. A. Soggin, Judges (OTL), 254.
n0701644: tn Heb “head.” By metonymy the hair of his head is meant.
n0701645: tn Heb “She began to humiliate him.” Rather than referring to some specific insulting action on Delilah’s part after Samson’s hair was shaved off, this statement probably means that she, through the devious actions just described, began the process of Samson’s humiliation which culminates in the following verses.
n0701647: tn The Hebrew adds, “from his sleep.” This has not been included in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n0701651: tn Most interpret this as a reference to Samson, but this seems premature, since v. 25 suggests he was not yet standing before them. Consequently some prefer to see this statement as displaced and move it to v. 25 (see C. F. Burney, Judges, 387). It seems more likely that the pronoun refers to an image of Dagon.
n0701655: tn Heb “the pillars upon which the house is founded.”
n0701657: tn The Hebrew has ‏אֲדֹנָי יֱהֹוִה‎ (ʾadonay yehovih, “Lord Yahweh”).
n0701658: tn Heb “so I can get revenge with one act of vengeance.”
n0701659: tn Heb “the pillars upon which the house was founded.”
n0701661: tn Heb “And the ones whom he killed in his death were many more than he killed in his life.”
n0701662: tn Heb “and all the house of his father.”
n0701663: tn Heb “and lifted him up and brought up.”
n070171: tn The words “You know” are supplied in the translation for clarification.
n070174: tn In the Hebrew text the statement, “but now I am giving it back to you,” appears at the end of v. 3 and is spoken by the mother. But v. 4 indicates that she did not give the money back to her son. Unless the statement is spoken by the woman to the Lord, it appears to be misplaced and fits much better in v. 2. It may have been accidentally omitted from a manuscript, written in the margin, and then later inserted in the wrong place in another manuscript.
n070176: tn Heb “dedicating, I dedicate.” In this case the emphatic infinitive absolute lends a mood of solemnity to the statement.
n070177: tn Heb “to the Lord from my hand for my son to make a carved image and cast metal image.” She cannot mean that she is now taking the money from her hand and giving it back to her son so he can make an image. Verses 4-6 indicate she took back the money and used a portion of it to hire a silversmith to make an idol for her son to use. The phrase “a carved image and cast metal image” is best taken as referring to two idols (see 18:17-18), even though the verb at the end of v. 4, ‏וַיְהִי‎ (vayehi, “and it was [in the house of Micah]”), is singular.
n070178: tn Heb “his mother.” The pronoun (“she”) has been substituted for the noun (“mother”) in the translation because of English style.
n070179: tn The Hebrew text has “and gave it.” The referent (the pieces of silver) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n0701712: sn Here an ephod probably refers to a priestly garment (cf. Exod 28:4-6).
n0701713: tn Heb “and he filled the hand of one of his sons and he became his priest.”
n0701714: tn Heb “Each was doing what was right in his [own] eyes.”
n0701716: tn Heb “There was a young man from Bethlehem of Judah, from the tribe of Judah, and he was a Levite, and he was temporarily residing there.”
n0701717: tn Heb “He came to the Ephraimite hill country, to Micah’s house, making his way.”
n0701718: tn Heb “And I am going to reside in a place I can find.”
n0701719: tn Heb “father.” “Father” is here a title of honor that suggests the priest will give advice and protect the interests of the family, primarily by divining God’s will in matters, perhaps through the use of the ephod. (See R. G. Boling, Judges [AB], 257; also Gen 45:8, where Joseph, who was a diviner and interpreter of dreams, is called Pharaoh’s “father,” and 2 Kgs 6:21; 13:14, where a prophet is referred to as a “father.” Note also 2 Kgs 8:9, where a king identifies himself as a prophet’s “son.” One of a prophet’s main functions was to communicate divine oracles. Cf. 2 Kgs 8:9ff.; 13:14-19).
n0701720: tn The Hebrew text expands with the phrase: “and the Levite went.” This only makes sense if taken with “to live” in the next verse. Apparently “the Levite went” and “the Levite agreed” are alternative readings which have been juxtaposed in the text.
n0701721: tn Heb “the young man became like one of his sons.”
n070182: tn Heb “because there had not fallen to them by that day in the midst of the tribes of Israel an inheritance.”
n070183: tn Heb “The Danites sent from their tribe five men, from their borders.”
n070185: tn Heb “They came to the Ephraimite hill country, to Micah’s house, and spent the night there.”
n070187: tn Heb “voice.” This probably means that “his speech was Judahite [i.e., southern] like their own, not Israelite [i.e., northern]” (R. G. Boling, Judges [AB], 263).
n0701810: tn Heb “He said to them, ‘Such and such Micah has done for me.’” Though the statement is introduced and presented, at least in part, as a direct quotation (note especially “for me”), the phrase “such and such” appears to be the narrator’s condensed version of what the Levite really said.
n0701812: tn Heb “so we can know if our way on which we are going will be successful.”
n0701814: tn Heb “In front of the Lord is your way in which you are going.”
n0701817: tn Heb “according to the custom of the Sidonians.”
n0701818: tn Heb “and there was no one humiliating anything in the land, one taking possession [by] force.”
n0701819: tc Heb “and a thing there was not to them with men.” Codex Alexandrinus (A) of the LXX and Symmachus read “Syria” here rather than the MT’s “men.” This reading presupposes a Hebrew Vorlage ‏אֲרָם‎ (ʾaram, “Aram,” i.e., Arameans) rather than the MT reading ‏אָדָם‎ (ʾadam). This reading is possibly to be preferred over the MT.
n0701823: tn Heb “Arise, and let us go up against them.”
n0701824: tc Codex Alexandrinus (A) of the LXX adds “we entered and walked around in the land as far as Laish and.”
n0701830: tn Heb “broad of hands,” an idiom meaning “wide on both sides.”
n0701831: tn Heb “a place where there is no lack of anything that is in the earth.”
n0701832: tn Heb “They journeyed from there, from the tribe of the Danites, from Zorah and from Eshtaol, 600 men, equipped with weapons of war.”
n0701833: tn Or “Mahaneh Dan”; the Hebrew term “Mahaneh” means “camp [of].” Many English versions retain the transliterated Hebrew expression, but cf. CEV “Dan’s Camp.”
n0701835: tc Codex Alexandrinus (A) of the LXX lacks the phrase “of Laish.”
n0701840: tn Heb “And the 600 men, equipped with the weapons of war…from the sons of Dan.”
n0701842: tn Heb “600 men, equipped with the weapons of war.”
n0701843: tn Heb “These went into Micah’s house and took.”
n0701844: tn See the note on the word “adviser” in 17:10.
n0701845: tn Heb “Is it better for you to be priest for the house of one man or for you to be priest for a tribe, for a clan in Israel?”
n0701846: tn Heb “and went into the midst of the people.”
n0701847: tn Heb “They turned and went and put the children, the cattle, and the possessions in front of them.”
n0701848: tn Heb “the men who were in the houses near Micah’s house.”
n0701849: tn Heb “they”; the referent (the Danites) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n0701850: tn Heb “What is this you say to me, ‘What to you?’”
n0701851: tn Heb “bitter in spirit.” This phrase is used in 2 Sam 17:8 of David and his warriors, who are compared to a bear robbed of her cubs.
n0701852: tn Heb “and you will gather up your life and the life of your house.”
n0701855: tn Heb “they”; the referent (the Danites) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n0701856: tn The Hebrew adds “with fire.” This has not been included in the translation for stylistic reasons, because it is redundant in English.
n0701857: tn Heb “it.” The Hebrew pronoun is feminine singular here, referring to the “city” (a grammatically feminine singular noun) mentioned in v. 27.
n0701859: tn Heb “and a thing there was not to them with men.”
n0701860: tn Heb “it.” The Hebrew pronoun is feminine singular here, referring to the “city” (a grammatically feminine singular noun) mentioned in v. 27.
n0701861: tn Heb “They”; the referent (the Danites) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n0701862: tn Heb “They called the name of the city Dan, after the name of Dan their father, who had been born to Israel.”
n0701865: tc Several ancient textual witnesses, including some LXX mss and the Vulgate, support the reading “Moses” (‏מֹשֶׁה‎, mosheh) here. Many Hebrew mss have a nun (‏נ‎) suspended above the name between the first two letters (‏מנשׁה‎), suggesting the name Manasseh (‏מְנַשֶּׁה‎, menasheh). This is probably a scribal attempt to protect Moses’ reputation. For discussion, see G. F. Moore, Judges (ICC), 401-2.
n0701868: tn Heb “the carved image that Micah had made.”
n070192: sn See the note on the word “concubine” in 8:31.
n070194: tn Heb “and his concubine.” The pronoun (“she”) has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n070195: tn Or “was unfaithful to him.” Many have understood the Hebrew verb ‏וַתִּזְנֶה‎ (vattizneh) as being from ‏זָנָה‎ (zanah, “to be a prostitute”), but it may be derived from a root meaning “to be angry; to hate” attested in Akkadian (see HALOT 275 s.v. II ‏זנה‎).
n070198: tn Heb “to speak to her heart to bring her back.”
n0701912: tn Heb “Sustain your heart [with] a bit of food.”
n0701913: tn Heb “And they sat and ate, the two of them together, and they drank.”
n0701914: tn Heb “Be willing and spend the night so that your heart might be good.”
n0701916: tn Heb “his father-in-law persuaded him and he again spent the night there.”
n0701917: tn Heb “Sustain your heart.” He is once more inviting him to stay for a meal.
n0701918: tn Heb “Wait until the declining of the day.”
n0701921: tn Heb “the day is sinking to become evening.”
n0701923: tn Heb “for your way and go to your tent.”
n0701927: tc Some ancient witnesses add “and his servant.”
n0701931: tn Heb “[in] which not any of the sons of Israel [are] here.”
n0701933: tn Heb “we will enter one of the places.”
n0701936: tn Heb “they turned aside there to enter to spend the night.”
n0701937: tn Heb “and he entered and sat down, and there was no one receiving them into the house to spend the night.”
n0701938: tn Heb “And look, an old man was coming from his work, from the field in the evening.”
n0701939: tn Heb “And the men of the place were Benjaminites.”
n0701941: tn Heb “he”; the referent (the Levite) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n0701943: tn Heb “I went to Bethlehem in Judah, but [to] the house of the Lord I am going.” The Hebrew text has “house of the Lord,” which might refer to the shrine at Shiloh. The LXX reads “to my house.”
n0701944: tn By calling his concubine the old man’s “female servant,” the Levite emphasizes their dependence on him for shelter.
n0701945: tc Some Hebrew mss and ancient witnesses read the singular, “your servant,” which would refer to the Levite. If one retains the plural, then both the Levite and his wife are in view. In either case the pronominal suffix emphasizes their dependence on the old man for shelter.
n0701950: tn Heb “the men of the city, men, the sons of wickedness.” The phrases are in apposition; the last phrase specifies what type of men they were. It is not certain if all the men of the city are in view, or just a group of troublemakers. In 20:5 the town leaders are implicated in the crime, suggesting that all the men of the city were involved. If so, the implication is that the entire male population of the town were good-for-nothings.
n0701951: tn The Hitpael verb form appears to have an iterative force here, indicating repeated action.
n0701952: tn Heb “know.” The expression ‏יָדַע‎ (yadaʿ) “to know” is a euphemism for sexual relations. Elsewhere NET employs the English euphemism “be intimate with” for this use of ‏יָדַע‎ (yadaʿ), but uses a different euphemism here because of the perverse overtones of force in this context. Their intent is to molest him, but their rhetoric tries to minimize their wickedness.
n0701953: tn Heb “his”; the referent (the visiting Levite) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n0701955: tn Heb “he”; the referent (the Levite) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n0701956: tn Heb “and he caused [her] to go outside to them.”
n0701958: tn The Hebrew term here translated “master,” is plural. The plural indicates degree here and emphasizes the Levite’s absolute sovereignty over the woman.
n0701959: tn Heb “The woman came at the turning of the morning and fell at the door of the house of the man where her master was until the light.”
n0701960: tn The Hebrew term here translated “master,” is plural. The plural indicates degree here and emphasizes the Levite’s absolute sovereignty over the woman.
n0701961: tn Heb “And the man took her on the donkey and arose and went to his place.”
n0701962: tn Heb “he carved her up by her bones into twelve pieces.”
n0701963: tn Heb “and he sent her through all the territory of Israel.”
n0701964: tn The words “the sight” are supplied in the translation for clarification.
n0701966: tc Codex Alexandrinus (A) of the (original) LXX has the following additional words: “And he instructed the men whom he sent out, ‘Thus you will say to every male Israelite: “There has never been anything like this from the day the Israelites left Egypt till the present day.”’”
n070201: sn Dan was located in the far north of the country, while Beer Sheba was located in the far south. This encompassed all the territory of the land of Canaan occupied by the Israelites.
n070202: sn The land of Gilead was on the eastern side of the Jordan River.
n070204: tn Heb “and the assembly was convened as one man.”
n070205: tn Heb “the cornerstones”; or “the supports.” The word is used of leaders in only three other texts—1 Sam 14:38; Isa 19:13; Zech 10:4.
n070206: tn The words “which numbered” are supplied in the translation for clarification.
n0702010: tn Heb “arose against me and surrounded against me the house at night.”
n0702011: tn Heb “her”; the referent is more naturally stated in English as “the pieces.”
n0702012: tn Heb “throughout all the territory of the inheritance of Israel.”
n0702014: tn Heb “Look, all of you sons of Israel.”
n0702015: tn Heb “give for yourselves a word and advice here.”
n0702019:
tn Heb “against her by lot.” The verb “we will go up” (‏נַעֲלֶה‎, naʿaleh) has probably been accidentally omitted before “against her” (‏עָלֶיהָ‎, ʿaleha).
sn As the lot dictates. The Israelite soldiers intended to cast lots to determine which tribe would lead the battle charge (see v. 18).
n0702021: tn Heb “to do at their arrival in Geba of Benjamin according to all the disgraceful [thing] which he [collective = “Benjamin”] did in Israel.” Here “Geba” must be an error for “Gibeah.”
n0702022: tn Heb “gathered at the city as one man, united.”
n0702023: tc The MT reads the plural, but surely the singular (which is supported by the LXX and Vulgate) is preferable here.
n0702024: tn Heb “What is this wicked thing which happened among you?”
n0702028: tn Heb “besides from the ones living in Gibeah they mustered 700 choice men.”
n0702031: tn Heb “700 choice men, bound/restricted in the right hand.” On the significance of the idiom, “bound/restricted in the right hand,” see the translator’s note on 3:15.
n0702032: tn “at a single hair and not miss.”
n0702035: tn Heb “They arose and went up to Bethel and asked God, and the Israelites said.”
n0702036: tn Heb “Who should go up for us first for battle against the sons of Benjamin?”
n0702038: tn Heb “the men of Israel.” The noun phrase has been replaced by the pronoun (“they”) in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n0702039: tn Heb “The sons of Benjamin came out of Gibeah and they struck down in Israel that day 22,000 men to the ground.”
n0702047: tn Heb “And Benjamin went out to meet them from Gibeah the second day, and they again struck down among the sons of Israel 18,000 men to the ground, all of these were wielding the sword.”
n0702060: tn Heb “and they were drawn away from the city.”
n0702063: tn The words “they struck down” are supplied in the translation for clarification.
n0702066: sn Verses 33-36a give a condensed account of the battle from this point on, while vv. 36b-48 offer a more detailed version of how the ambush contributed to Gibeah’s defeat.
n0702068: tn Heb “And they did not know that touching against them was disaster.”
n0702069: tn Heb “And the sons of Israel struck down in Benjamin that day 25,100 men, all of these wielding the sword.”
n0702073: tn Or “deployed.” The verb normally means “to lead” or “to draw.”
n0702074: tn Heb “they”; the referent (the men hiding in ambush) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n0702076: tn Heb “And Benjamin began to strike down wounded ones among the men of Israel.”
n0702077: tn The words “they struck down” are supplied in the translation for clarification.
n0702078: tn Heb “Benjamin turned after him and, look, the whole city went up toward the sky.”
n0702082: tn Heb “and those from the cities were striking them down in their midst.”
n0702083: tc The translation assumes the reading ‏מִנּוֹחָה‎ (minnokhah, “from Nohah”; cf. 1 Chr 8:2) rather than the MT’s ‏מְנוּחָה‎ (menukhah, “resting place”).
n0702085: tc Heb “unto the opposite of Gibeah toward the east.” Gibeah cannot be correct here, since the Benjaminites retreated from there toward the desert and Rimmon (see v. 45). A slight emendation yields the reading “Geba.”
n0702086: tn Heb “they”; the referent (the rest [of the Benjaminites]) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n0702087: tn Heb “and they”; the referent (the Israelites) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n0702088: tn Heb “gleaned.” The word is an agricultural term which pictures Israelites picking off the Benjaminites as easily as one picks grapes from the vine.
n0702090: sn The number given here (25,000 sword-wielding Benjaminites) is an approximate figure; v. 35 gives the more exact number (25,100). According to v. 15, the Benjaminite army numbered 26,700 (26,000 + 700). The figures in vv. 35 (rounded in vv. 44-46) and 47 add up to 25,700. What happened to the other 1,000 men? The most reasonable explanation is that they were killed during the first two days of fighting. G. F. Moore (Judges [ICC], 429) and C. F. Burney (Judges, 475) reject this proposal, arguing that the narrator is too precise and concerned about details to omit such a fact. However, the account of the first two days’ fighting emphasizes Israel’s humiliating defeat. To speak of Benjaminite casualties would diminish the literary effect. In vv. 35, 44-47 the narrator’s emphasis is the devastating defeat that Benjamin experienced on this final day of battle. To mention the earlier days’ casualties at this point is irrelevant to his literary purpose. He allows readers who happen to be concerned with such details to draw conclusions for themselves.
n0702091: tn Heb “So all the ones who fell from Benjamin were 25,000 men, wielding the sword, in that day, all of these men of strength.
n0702093: tc The translation is based on the reading ‏מֵעִיר מְתִים‎ (meʿir metim, “from a city of men,” i.e., “an inhabited city”), rather than the reading ‏מֵעִיר מְתֹם‎ (meʿir metom, “from a city of soundness”) found in the Leningrad Codex (L).
n0702094: tn Heb “Also all the cities that were found they set on fire.”
n070212: tn Heb “and they lifted up their voice[s] and wept with great weeping.” Both the cognate accusative ‏בְּכִי‎ (bekhi, “weeping”) and the attributive adjective ‏גָדוֹל‎ (gadol, “great”) emphasize their degree of sorrow.
n070215: tn Heb “A great oath there was concerning the one who did not go up before the Lord at Mizpah, saying, ‘He must surely be put to death.’”
n070218: tn Heb “What should we do for them, for the remaining ones, concerning wives?”
n070219: tn Heb “Look, no one had come to the camp from Jabesh Gilead to the assembly.”
n0702115: tn Heb “And this is the thing that you will do.”
n0702116: tn Heb “a knower of the bed of a male.” The verb ‏יָדָע‎ (yadaʿ) “to know,” “be intimate with,” is used as a euphemism for sexual relations.
n0702117: tc Some Greek witnesses (notably Codex Vaticanus [B]) add the words, “‘But the virgins you should keep alive.’ And they did so.” These additional words, which probably represent the original Hebrew text, can be retroverted: ‏וְאֶת־הַבְּתוּלוֹת תְּחַיּוּ וַיַּעֲשׂוּ כֵן‎ (veʾet habbetulot tekhayyu vayyaʿasu khen). It is likely that a scribe’s eye jumped from the ‏ו‎ (vav) on ‏וְאֶת‎ (veʾet) to the initial vav of v. 11, accidentally leaving out the intervening letters. The present translation is based on this reconstruction.
n0702118: tn Heb “who were not knowers of a man by the bed of a male.” The verb ‏יָדַע‎ (yadaʿ) “to know,” or “to be intimate with,” acts as a euphemism for sexual relations, which is further clarified by reference to a man’s bed.
n0702119: tn Heb “And all the assembly sent and spoke to the sons of Benjamin who were at the cliff of Rimmon and they proclaimed to them peace.”
n0702120: tn Heb “they”; the referent (the Israelites) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n0702121: tn Heb “but they did not find for them enough.”
n0702123: tn Heb “had made a gaping hole in.” The narrator uses imagery that compares Israel to a wall that has been breached.
n0702125: tn Heb “What should we do for the remaining ones concerning wives?”
n0702126: tn The Hebrew text has “and they said” at the beginning of the verse. For stylistic reasons the translation treats v. 17 as a continuation of the remarks of the leaders in v. 16.
n0702127: tn Heb “An inheritance for the remnant belonging to Benjamin, and a tribe from Israel will not be wiped away.” The first statement lacks a verb. Some prefer to emend the text to read, “How can an inheritance remain for the remnant of Benjamin?”
n0702128: tn Heb “But we are not able to give to them wives from our daughters.”
n0702134: tc The (original) LXX and Vulgate read “to you.”
n0702135: tn The words “and let them be” are supplied in the translation for clarification.
n0702136:
tn Heb “for we did not take each his wife in battle.”
sn Through battle. This probably refers to the battle against Jabesh Gilead, which only produced 400 of the 600 wives needed.
n0702137: tn This sentence is not in the Hebrew text. It is supplied in the translation to clarify the logic of the statement.
n0702138: tc Heb “You did not give to them, now you are guilty.” The MT as it stands makes little sense. It is preferable to emend ‏לֹא‎ (loʾ, “not”) to ‏לוּא‎ (luʾ, “if”). This particle introduces a purely hypothetical condition, “If you had given to them [but you didn’t].” See G. F. Moore, Judges (ICC), 453-54.
n0702140: tn Heb “And they took wives according to their number from the dancing girls whom they abducted.”
n0702145:
tn Heb “Each was doing what was right in his [own] eyes.”
sn Each man did what he considered to be right. The Book of Judges closes with this note, which summarizes the situation of the Israelite tribes during this period.
